thread: Who is the newer grain seller on the road, and does he matter?

evidence.clue: Mira remembers adults arguing about grain, price, and a newer trader before the village fell.
Paret reports a younger seller cutting under established traders with cheap sacks and changing the look of his wagon.
The living village repeats the same pattern: a new grain supplier arrived three months ago with lower prices, changing wagon paint, and a face witnesses cannot hold onto clearly.
Sylvanas recovers one more usable detail from memory: the trader rode an empty wagon when grain should have weighed it down and offered the odd farewell "May the stars keep your road."
A second forgettable road man uses the same farewell — 'May the stars keep your road' — making the phrase itself a repeat clue even though the identity behind it remains unproven.
Cyndia hears the repeated farewell — "May the stars keep your road" — from a retreating hooded figure leaving the trap site.
The farewell “May the stars keep your road” surfaces a fourth time from an ordinary camp aide during Hearthglen’s departure rush.
Sylvanas also notices a dark ring on the aide’s hand, giving the repeating pattern its first new physical marker in several chapters.
A fleeing grain carter in Stratholme gives the route to the inner wall and then says “May the stars keep your road” as if it were habitual ordinary luck.
This is the fifth known recurrence of the farewell, and it appears under immediate urban survival pressure rather than in reflective conversation.

readerKnows: The repeating farewell has now surfaced five times across villages, trap ground, army movement, and urban flight. Chapter 17 makes the pattern harder to file as coincidence and still impossible to explain cleanly.
povCharacterKnows: Sylvanas now knows the phrase can surface from a frightened carter who also offers practical route intelligence, which widens the thread beyond simple roadside memory or camp coincidence.
notes: Advanced again in chapter 17. The fifth recurrence comes from an ordinary grain carter under immediate survival pressure, which strengthens the pattern precisely because he sounds uncurated.
